titulo: World Coffee Statistics Database
texto: World Coffee Statistics Database | International Coffee Organization World Coffee Statistics Database WORLD COFFEE STATISTICS DATABASE Launched on 31 January 2022, it is the repository of one of the richest/largest coffee data sets in the world, housing the ICO’s coffee data. Trade, prices, production, consumption, inventories are the main focus of the WCSD, with data available in a monthly format starting from October 1963. The key feature of the database is its ability to generate reports on a disaggregated basis, giving analysts and users of ICO’s coffee statistics a simple control and access to its data set. Access to the WCSD is open only to either the Members of the ICO or subscribers to one of the Statistical Publications*. Visit the database MONTHLY TRADE STATISTICS (MTS) The main vehicle of the ICO in the dissemination of the latest volume and trend of the global coffee trade. It covers the 6 immediate past months of exports of all exporting countries, as well as the imports a
textoCompleto: World Coffee Statistics Database | International Coffee Organization World Coffee Statistics Database WORLD COFFEE STATISTICS DATABASE Launched on 31 January 2022, it is the repository of one of the richest/largest coffee data sets in the world, housing the ICO’s coffee data. Trade, prices, production, consumption, inventories are the main focus of the WCSD, with data available in a monthly format starting from October 1963. The key feature of the database is its ability to generate reports on a disaggregated basis, giving analysts and users of ICO’s coffee statistics a simple control and access to its data set. Access to the WCSD is open only to either the Members of the ICO or subscribers to one of the Statistical Publications*. Visit the database MONTHLY TRADE STATISTICS (MTS) The main vehicle of the ICO in the dissemination of the latest volume and trend of the global coffee trade. It covers the 6 immediate past months of exports of all exporting countries, as well as the imports and re-exports of major non-producing countries. 12 months of exports of Arabica and Robusta for all exporting countries are also covered within the MTS. The subscription to the MTS is linked with access to the respective areas of the WCSD. QUATERLY STATISTICAL BULLETIN (QSB) Comprehensive coverage of the global coffee industry is the focus of the QSB. Trade (volume and value), production, consumption, inventories, prices (CIP, prices to growers and retail prices) and economic data are found within the QSB. The data coverage is global, with individual breakouts for all exporters and major consumers, and where appropriate grouped into regions. Data are in a combination of monthly, quarterly and annual for the past 6 years. The subscription to the QSB is linked with access to the respective areas of the WCSD. COFFEE REPORT AND OUTLOOK (CRO) The Coffee Report and Outlook is a new bi-annual report providing a snapshot of the past and current coffee years’ production and consumption. Annex contains a comprehensive production and consumption data for five historic years and current year data for 192 consuming countries and 54 producing countries delivered by email. SUBSCRIBER PACKAGE PRICING All Inclusive Package Publications Package Monthly Trade Statistics (MTS) Quarterly Statistical Bulletin (QSB) Coffee Report and Outlook (CRO) Daily Prices Customized data service All of the Publications Package and Services £2500 ( purchase ) Subscription to the three publications described below £1500 ( purchase ) Monthly delivery via email of ICO’s flagship regular trade statistical publication, and access to the World Coffee Statistics Database (WCSD) £750 ( purchase ) Quarterly delivery via email of trade, prices, production, consumption and consumption per capita data and access to the World Coffee Statistics Database (WCSD) linked with QSB £600 ( purchase ) Delivery via email of the annex, containing a comprehensive snapshot of production and consumption data for five historic years and current year data for 192 consuming countries and 54 producing countries. £500 ( purchase ) ICO’s Composite Indicator Price, four group indicators and regional market split £1200 ( purchase ) Limited number of customized data retrievals and deliveries via email Minimum £250 per request, but available free to “Publications /Services” Package subscribers ( purchase ) STATISTICS DOCUMENT STORE To purchase specific reports from the ICO Statistics section please visit our store . titulo: Public Market Information
texto: Public Market Information | International Coffee Organization Public Market Information DAILY PRICES Established in the 1960s, the ICO Composite Indicator Price (I-CIP) is one of the key reference prices of the coffee industry in the world, used by both public, private and academic sectors to assess their costs or plan budgets. I-CIP is split into four groups of coffee and are published daily on the ICO’s website, uploaded by 10.00am GMT. Current Month of I-CIP I-CIP Previous Months of I-CIP April 2026 March 2026 February 2026 January 2026 December 2025 November 2025 October 2025 September 2025 August 2025 July 2025 June 2025 May 2025 Please Note: The Composite and Group Indicator prices as well as Europe and US market prices are available in an excel format on request from stats@ico.org . The data is available for free to Members of the ICO, intergovernmental organizations and academic researchers. Others users of the data may email us for a quote. SUPPLY, DEMAND AND TRADE Coffee Repo

I-CIP is split into four groups of coffee and are published daily on the ICO’s website, uploaded by 10.00am GMT. titulo: Coffee Market Report
texto: Coffee Market Report – Statistics Section | International Coffee Organization Coffee Market Report ABOUT The Coffee Market Report (CMR) is the flagship publication of the ICO for analysis of recent events in the global coffee industry, with a focus on price movements, trade and supply/demand balances. The CMR is the monthly reference benchmark publication on trends in the coffee sector reported by generalist and specialized coffee media, available free-of-charge, and found on the ICO website or delivered via email. titulo: The Coffee Public-Private Task Force
texto: The Coffee Public-Private Task Force | International Coffee Organization The Coffee Public-Private Task Force ABOUT THE TASK FORCE MISSION The Coffee Public-Private Task Force was established by the International Coffee Organization in 2019, following the signature of the “London Declaration” by 12 private sector companies, and upon the request of the International Coffee Council at its 125 th Session. The Task Force is designed to build common ground for a global partnership between the coffee industry and governments, guided by the overriding vision of the sustainability of the sector and prosperity for grower communities. textoCompleto: The Coffee Public-Private Task Force | International Coffee Organization The Coffee Public-Private Task Force ABOUT THE TASK FORCE MISSION The Coffee Public-Private Task Force was established by the International Coffee Organization in 2019, following the signature of the “London Declaration” by 12 private sector companies, and upon the request of the International Coffee Council at its 125 th Session. The Task Force is designed to build common ground for a global partnership between the coffee industry and governments, guided by the overriding vision of the sustainability of the sector and prosperity for grower communities. OBJECTIVES The overall objective of the Task Force is to build consensus between public and the private actors on priority issues and actions to be implemented in order to ensure a sustainable and fair global coffee sector at the local and global levels. To this end, collaborative public-private partnerships aim to achieve complementary specific objectives: 1. Designing methodologies and strategies to close the income gap for farmers towards prosperity; 2. Enhancing sector-wide transparency on a common understanding of the actual production and the relative efficiency of value transmission; 3. Promoting enabling policies in exporting and importing countries, facilitating national public-private platforms; 4. Driving the resilience of coffee communities by using a landscape approach; 5. Coordinating interventions at the sector level and ensuring full integration of the gender and youth agenda. ACHIEVEMENTS The technical workstreams have been activated and started providing the expertise required to close key knowledge gaps, elevate existing research and begin interventions in selected ICO exporting countries on: 1. Defining Living income benchmarks and closing the gap strategies; 2. Establishing cost of production and efficiency gap data; 3. Setting up national dialogue platforms and ensuring viability of regulatory policies; 4. Carrying out landscape assessments to inform strategies on territorial, not exclusively at farm level; 5. Increasing membership from private sector and active participation of public and private sector and observers (coffee stakeholders and development partners); 6. Increasing financial / in-kind contributions from Members, observers and international development cooperation; 7. Linking development with EU, UN agencies and institutions; 8. Building consensus model of Task Force so that it proves relevant, important and recognized by actors of the coffee sector. OUR MEMBERS Since its inception in 2019, the CPPTF has started to attract increased interest from the private sector, with three additional companies having joined. Moreover, in order to maintain the overall balance of public-private participants, more ICO Members have also been incorporated. Private Sector Public Sector ECOM Trading Illycaffè S.p.A. JDE Peet’s N.V. Lavazza S.p.A. Louis Dreyfus Company Nestlé S.A. Neumann Kaffee Gruppe Ofi Starbucks Coffee Company Sucafina Sucden Tchibo Volcafe Indian Coffee Trust National Coffee Association Global Coffee Platform Sustainable Coffee Challenge Brazil Colombia Côte d’Ivoire Ethiopia European Union Honduras India Indonesia Kenya Mexico El Salvador Papua New Guinea Peru Rwanda Switzerland Togo Vietnam Rwanda Switzerland Togo View Member Bios ROADMAP 2020-2030 Download Roadmap TECHNICAL WORKSTREAMS Technical Workstreams are organized according to the thematic areas as identified by the Resolution 465/London Declaration and are expected to produce technical inputs and reports to support the implementation of Task Force commitments and concrete actions. Click below for information and updates on the work of the technical working group. Technical Workstreams Global Knowledge Hub EVENTS & MEETINGS 7th CEO & Global Leaders Forum (CGLF) We are preparing the 7th CEO & Global Leaders Forum (CGLF), which will take place in Honduras on the week starting on 13 October. The CGLF is the highest-level public-private dialogue platform in the coffee sector, bringing together companies and governments from producing and consuming countries. If you would like to learn more about the CGLF, check out the video from last year’s Forum (6th CGLF). titulo: Coffee Development Report
texto: Coffee Development Report | International Coffee Organization Coffee Development Report BEYOND COFFEE: TOWARDS A CIRCULAR ECONOMY IN COFFEE The ICO is delighted to announce the launch of our flagship Coffee Development Report (CDR) 2022-23, an in-depth examination of the circular economy’s transformative potential within the coffee sector. The CDR 2023-2023 was unveiled at the 6th CEOs and Global Leaders Forum (CGLF) in September 2024, and the edition of the full report recently launched features collaborative policy recommendations to promote circular economy practices across the coffee value chain, delivering cutting-edge research, fresh data, and sector analysis enriched with practical case studies for a sustainable coffee future. Produced through landmark collaboration with the International Trade Centre (ITC), the Center for Circular Economy in Coffee (C4CEC), Lavazza Foundation, and Politecnico di Torino, with contributions from University College London, this CDR edition reflect
textoCompleto: Coffee Development Report | International Coffee Organization Coffee Development Report BEYOND COFFEE: TOWARDS A CIRCULAR ECONOMY IN COFFEE The ICO is delighted to announce the launch of our flagship Coffee Development Report (CDR) 2022-23, an in-depth examination of the circular economy’s transformative potential within the coffee sector. The CDR 2023-2023 was unveiled at the 6th CEOs and Global Leaders Forum (CGLF) in September 2024, and the edition of the full report recently launched features collaborative policy recommendations to promote circular economy practices across the coffee value chain, delivering cutting-edge research, fresh data, and sector analysis enriched with practical case studies for a sustainable coffee future. Produced through landmark collaboration with the International Trade Centre (ITC), the Center for Circular Economy in Coffee (C4CEC), Lavazza Foundation, and Politecnico di Torino, with contributions from University College London, this CDR edition reflects the work of a diverse global multi-stakeholder group under the ITC Coffee Guide Network. titulo: International Coffee Day (ICD)
texto: International Coffee Day (ICD) | International Coffee Organization International Coffee Day (ICD) ABOUT INTERN ATIONAL COFFEE DAY 1 October is International Coffee Day. Celebrate with the 77 Member States of the ICO and dozens of coffee associations from around the world. International Coffee Day is a celebration of the coffee sector’s diversity, quality and passion. It is an opportunity for coffee lovers to share their love of the beverage and support the millions of farmers whose livelihoods depend on the aromatic crop. Many countries around the world celebrate their own national coffee days at various dates throughout the year. In March 2014, the Member States of the ICO agreed to organize International Coffee Day on 1 October to create a single day of celebration for coffee lovers around the world. Access ICD Website ICD 2024 On International Coffee Day 2024, the International Coffee Organization Invites the Coffee Sector to Embrace Collaboration for Collective Action with the Mess
textoCompleto: International Coffee Day (ICD) | International Coffee Organization International Coffee Day (ICD) ABOUT INTERN ATIONAL COFFEE DAY 1 October is International Coffee Day. Celebrate with the 77 Member States of the ICO and dozens of coffee associations from around the world. International Coffee Day is a celebration of the coffee sector’s diversity, quality and passion. It is an opportunity for coffee lovers to share their love of the beverage and support the millions of farmers whose livelihoods depend on the aromatic crop. Many countries around the world celebrate their own national coffee days at various dates throughout the year. In March 2014, the Member States of the ICO agreed to organize International Coffee Day on 1 October to create a single day of celebration for coffee lovers around the world. Access ICD Website ICD 2024 On International Coffee Day 2024, the International Coffee Organization Invites the Coffee Sector to Embrace Collaboration for Collective Action with the Message: ‘Coffee, Your Daily Ritual, Our Shared Journey” As the coffee year starts on 1st October; and with 2025 being designated by the United Nations General Assembly as the International Year of Cooperatives; the ICO has been inspired to make “Collaboration” the central theme for International Coffee Day 2024 (ICD2024). Given that most of the 2025 campaign year will coincide with the coffee year, this theme will emphasize the importance of embracing collaboration for collective action and its role in achieving sustainable development in the global coffee sector. This annual event will serve as a reminder that our favourite brew is the result of extensive, often unseen collaboration among countless actors across the global coffee value chain, all working together to deliver that perfect cup each day. To help share and spread the campaign message, under the slogan, “Coffee, your daily ritual, our shared jurney”, we are pleased to introduce the newly redesigned website, providing a comprehensive overview of this year’s campaign, dedicated to raising awareness about the importance of collaboration within the coffee sector and its potential to inspire collective action on a global scale. The ICD2024 campaign offers to the public a broad range of assets, from videos, posters to postcards, available in various languages, that visually portray examples of collaboration, demonstrating how partnerships extend beyond what is commonly known or perceived. Your participation is key to making International Coffee Day a success. Use the hashtags #ICD2024 and/or #EmbraceCollaboration to join the conversation. And, of course, don’t forget to enjoy your favourite coffee brew! Happy International Coffee Day! Website ICD 2023 In 2023, the International Coffee Organization (ICO) launched the #CoffeePeople campaign in partnership with ILO (International Labour Organization), a collaborative effort aimed at promoting safer and healthier working conditions for coffee workers globally. The campaign, officially launched on International Coffee Day (1 October 2023), was embraced by the International Coffee Organization (ICO), with its Council designating “Joining forces for a safer coffee supply chain” as the official theme of its 2023 promotional activities. Key Messages The campaign emphasized several key messages: 1. Every coffee worker deserves a safe and healthy workplace. 2. Work-related injuries and diseases in coffee production are preventable. 3. Safe working conditions are essential for decent work. 4. Recognizing gender perspectives is crucial, given that 70% of coffee labourers are women. 5. Collective action and social dialogue are necessary to find solutions. Social Media Challenge On September 27th, the Action launched a social media challenge for coffee workers. We asked coffee stakeholders: “What is the secret to great coffee?” On October 1st, thousands of people around the world posted on social media a selfie of themselves with a coffee cup and revealed the response: “The secret to great coffee is people! That’s why I dedicate this coffee to the workers who produce it, and I join the ILO’s Vision Zero Fund to protect their safety and health. Together, we can make a difference for #CoffeePeople”, challenging colleagues, friends and family to do the same Watch the video ICD 2022 Committed to coffee farmers’ prosperity, reducing the coffee industry’s impact on the environment and mitigating climate change ICD 2022 celebrates all those working hard to make the three billion delicious coffee cups we enjoy every day around the world and stresses that transforming waste in the coffee sector into new products and alternative energy options creates important income and job opportunities and reduces production costs ( read the brochure ). The coffee sector is a crucial anchor for development and a driver of prosperity and sustainability. Producing and drinking coffee helps to make the world a better place by generating jobs and income in over 60 coffee producing countries and for millions of people worldwide engaged with the coffee sector (not to mention happiness for millions of coffee lovers!). Generating and sharing innovative and circular solutions for the global coffee value chain will foster economic, social and environmental sustainability from seed to cup, farmers to baristas and producers to conscious consumers, producers and retailers. Transforming waste into new products and energy, generating new jobs and income opportunities “Recycling alone will not save us.” Circular economy is a “bigger idea” — a significant restructuring that forces us to rethink how we’ve done things since the rise of the first steam engine”, Ellen MacArthur Foundation CEO Andrew Morlet, 2020. A circular coffee economy and solutions will help farmers, governments, businesses and consumers work together to create better jobs and income, reduce pollution and fight climate change. Implementing sustainable circular models in the coffee sector and digitalizing key processes to reinvent and make the coffee value chain sustainable and resilient. Coffee is both one of the most widely consumed beverages in the world and one of the most important internationally traded commodities. Let’s transform and reinvent the global coffee value chain to generate a fair and win-win economic transaction, particularly for coffee farmers; Let’s integrate the circular business model and solutions in the coffee sector, along with digital tools. We must move away from the traditional coffee business model that has become increasingly unsustainable for coffee producers and the environment, with huge waste production, high energy consumption and limited to no usage of disposals, and head towards inclusive circular models that reward sustainability, transparency, quality and hard work. ICD 2021 Supporting the Next Generation The International Coffee Organization (ICO) is excited to announce the continuation of ‘Coffee’s Next Generation’ as the focus for the International Coffee Day 2021 (1st October). The covid-19 pandemic is a global health crisis with extreme economic consequences that has also created an unprecedented situation in the coffee sector, negatively impacting both supply and demand in parallel for the first time. Even before covid-19, farmers were already struggling due to low coffee prices and global and environmental challenges and threats. In light of the cumulative effects of the coffee price crisis and the covid-19 pandemic, not only is there a serious threat to the livelihoods of coffee farmers today but also a dramatic risk to the future of coffee tomorrow. An increasing number of young people in coffee-farming households are moving away from ‘the family business’ to other locations and jobs that they see as more progressive and lucrative for their future. Furthermore, many of the jobs often held by young people across the coffee value chain have recently been lost. International Coffee Day is a

titulo: World Coffee Conference
texto: World Coffee Conference (WCC) | International Coffee Organization World Coffee Conference (WCC) ABOUT THE CONFERENCE Every four to five years, the International Coffee Organization holds a high-level World Coffee Conference to enable discussion around critical topics for the global coffee sector. The first four Conferences took place in the United Kingdom (2001), Brazil (2005), Guatemala (2010), and Ethiopia (2016). 5TH WCC 5th World Coffee Conference Bangalore, India (23 September to 1 October 2023) The world coffee community will gather in Bangalore for four days of engaging debate at the 5th World Coffee Conference, hosted by the Government of India and in connection with the G20 under the country’s presidency. The 75 Member states of the International Coffee Organization (ICO) and top-level experts, including coffee growers and representatives from government, the private sector, development partners, as well as civil society and academia, will debate on coffee market trends,
textoCompleto: World Coffee Conference (WCC) | International Coffee Organization World Coffee Conference (WCC) ABOUT THE CONFERENCE Every four to five years, the International Coffee Organization holds a high-level World Coffee Conference to enable discussion around critical topics for the global coffee sector. The first four Conferences took place in the United Kingdom (2001), Brazil (2005), Guatemala (2010), and Ethiopia (2016). 5TH WCC 5th World Coffee Conference Bangalore, India (23 September to 1 October 2023) The world coffee community will gather in Bangalore for four days of engaging debate at the 5th World Coffee Conference, hosted by the Government of India and in connection with the G20 under the country’s presidency. The 75 Member states of the International Coffee Organization (ICO) and top-level experts, including coffee growers and representatives from government, the private sector, development partners, as well as civil society and academia, will debate on coffee market trends, technology and best practices for building a circular coffee economy, regenerative agriculture, sustainability through consumption and access to finance. The WCC 2023 will also integrate the 5 th CEOs and Global Leaders Forum, a coffee technology fair and will be held alongside the 136 th Session of the International Coffee Council. Video 4TH WCC 4th World Coffee Conference Addis Ababa, Ethiopia (6 to 8 March 2016) The coffee community from Africa and around the world gathered in the Ethiopian capital for two days of engaging debate at the 4th World Coffee Conference, hosted by the Government of Ethiopia. The 77 Member Countries of the International Coffee Organization (ICO) and over 900 attendees, including coffee growers and representatives from government, the private sector, and international agencies participated in a range of discussions related to the theme of the Conference, ‘Nurturing coffee culture and diversity’. Programme | Photos: Flickr , Dropbox , .zip file | Videos | Review of themes 3TH WCC 3rd World Coffee Conference Guatemala City, Guatemala (26 to 28 February 2010) The ICO World Coffee Conference 2010 took place in Guatemala from 26 to 28 February 2010, chaired by the President of Guatemala, H.E. Mr. Alvaro Colom Caballeros. It brought together over 1,400 coffee growers and representatives from government, the private sector and international agencies from the 76 Member countries of the ICO. The Conference was inaugurated by the President of Guatemala, with the participation of the President of Honduras, H.E. Mr Porfirio Lobo. The President of El Salvador, H.E. Mr Carlos Mauricio Funes, participated in the Closing Ceremony. Also in attendance at the Opening Ceremony were the Secretary-General of UNCTAD, the Managing Director of the Common Fund for Commodities, the President of Anacafe, the ICO Executive Director, the Chairman of the International Coffee Council and Ministers of Agriculture from ICO Member countries. The main theme was “Coffee for the future: towards a sustainable coffee sector” and over 30 high-level speakers spoke on issues ranging from changes and trends in world supply and demand to environmental and social sustainability. The preliminary conclusions of the Conference were presented at the closing ceremony on 28 February 2010. The International Coffee Council reviewed the conclusions and outcomes of the Conference at its 105th Session from 20-24 September 2010. Programme l Review of themes 2nd WCC 2nd World Coffee Conference Salvador, Brazil (23 to 25 September 2005) The 2nd World Coffee Conference took place in Salvador, Brazil from 23 to 25 September 2005, chaired by Mr. Roberto Rodrigues, the Minister of Agriculture of Brazil. It brought together around 1200 coffee growers and representatives from government, the private sector and international agencies. Almost half the delegates came from overseas, from 65 countries. The Conference was inaugurated by the President of Brazil, Mr. Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva, in the presence of the President of Colombia, H.E. Mr. Alvaro Uribe, the Governor of Bahia and the ICO Executive Director. The main theme was “Lessons emerging from the crisis: New paths for the coffee sector`and over 20 high-level speakers spoke on issues ranging from coffee policies in a market economy to how to develop a sustainable coffee economy. The Chairman presented a preliminary summary of the Conference to the Council at its 94th Session from 27 to 29 September 2005, and presented the final report of the Conference to the Council in May 2006. Copies of a CD-Rom with detailed information about the Conference, including the programme, presentations and Conference conclusions, were distributed to Members attending the 95th Session of the Council (copies are available on request to ICO Members). A document reviewing themes raised at the Conference was circulated for use in discussions on the future of the 2001 Agreement (document EB-3901/05 Rev. 1). Review of themes l Summary report 1st WCC 1st World Coffee Conference London, UK (17 to 19 May 2001) The 1st World Coffee Conference took place in London from 17 to 19 May 2001. The Conference was chaired by Dr. Jorge Cárdenas, of Colombia, General Manager of the Federación Nacional de Cafeteros de Colombia, and brought together over 450 leaders and decision-makers from coffee producing countries, major private sector companies and many other organizations dealing with coffee. Delegates heard presentations on a wide range of topics, and many ideas for resolving the current crisis of low prices were put forward. New data on the positive effects on health of coffee consumption were also released during the Conference at a press conference on 18 May. The Council subsequently approved Resolution 400 urging follow up of the ideas presented at the Conference. A World Coffee Conference Programme Committee met and put forward a number of recommendations which were approved by the Council in May 2002 and incorporated into the ICO five-year Plan of Action. 222 Gray’s Inn Road, London WC1X 8HB Tel: +44 (0)20 7612 0600 Fax: +44 (0)20 7612 0630 Email: info@ico.org (c) International Coffee Organization Follow Follow Follow Follow Follow About the ICO Factsheet FAQs Documents Contact Us Global Knowledge Hub Statistics CPPTF

titulo: How Turkish coffee earned its place in specialty coffee
texto: How Turkish coffee earned its place in specialty coffee - Perfect Daily Grind --> ESPAÑOL ENGLISH PORTUGUÊS English Español Português Search for: Search Button SUBSCRIBE Skip to content Perfect Daily Grind > Brewing Essentials > How Turkish coffee earned its place in specialty coffee Share: Estimated reading time: 7 minutes Key takeaways The cezve is one of the worlds oldest brewing methods still used today. Turkish coffee culture was designated as an Intangible Cultural Heritage of UNESCO in 2013. Specialty coffee is rediscovering the cezve s body and flavour complexity. The World Cezve/Ibrik Championship has brought global recognition to the brewing method. A brewing method more than five centuries old is finding a new audience among baristas and specialty coffee enthusiasts worldwide. The cezve , a small, long-handled pot also known as an ibrik or Turkish coffee pot, has long been central to coffee culture not only in Turkey and the Balkans but across the Middle East, North Africa,
temas: cafe,especialidad,mercado
textoCompleto: How Turkish coffee earned its place in specialty coffee - Perfect Daily Grind --> ESPAÑOL ENGLISH PORTUGUÊS English Español Português Search for: Search Button SUBSCRIBE Skip to content Perfect Daily Grind > Brewing Essentials > How Turkish coffee earned its place in specialty coffee Share: Estimated reading time: 7 minutes Key takeaways The cezve is one of the worlds oldest brewing methods still used today. Turkish coffee culture was designated as an Intangible Cultural Heritage of UNESCO in 2013. Specialty coffee is rediscovering the cezve s body and flavour complexity. The World Cezve/Ibrik Championship has brought global recognition to the brewing method. A brewing method more than five centuries old is finding a new audience among baristas and specialty coffee enthusiasts worldwide. The cezve , a small, long-handled pot also known as an ibrik or Turkish coffee pot, has long been central to coffee culture not only in Turkey and the Balkans but across the Middle East, North Africa, and parts of Eastern Europe. Now it’s attracting renewed interest from the specialty coffee industry, which has more commonly championed modern brewing techniques and automation. “Take a small coffee pot, add water, finely ground coffee, stir, and let the mixture boil up just to the point where the grounds rise up to form a thick head of foam,” says Mehmet Kurukahveci, the grandson of the founder of Kurukahveci Mehmet Efendi , founded in 1871. “Then slowly pour into small cups. Wait a little for the fines to settle, then enjoy a rich, satisfying brew.” The recent embrace of traditional coffee preparation methods is significant. The cezve offers body and flavour complexity not comparable to most brewing methods, securing its place on the international stage. The World Cezve/Ibrik Championship , recently held at World of Coffee Dubai 2026, draws competitors from dozens of countries and has raised the brewing methods profile considerably in specialty coffee. For an industry that has spent decades looking forward, the renewed focus on one of coffees oldest traditions marks a notable shift. Thanks to the cezve ’s deep cultural and historical roots, as well as its emphasis on ritual, it has earned its place in specialty coffee. You may also like our article on why specialty coffee is growing in Turkey . A history of Turkish coffee Long before paper filters or espresso machines, there was the cezve , with origins dating back almost half a millennium . Historical records place the earliest cultivation of coffee in Yemen in the mid-15th century. Arabica, grown just across the Red Sea from its wild origins in the Ethiopian highlands, was first adopted by Sufi dervishes, who used the drink during long nightly religious ceremonies. As the Ottoman Empire expanded into the region, it carried coffee back to Istanbul, where it would take on an entirely new significance. By the 16th century, the coffeehouse had become a defining feature of daily life in Ottoman Turkey. Open to merchants, scholars, and citizens alike, these establishments functioned as centres of conversation and hospitality. “It was here, in Istanbul, that Western traders, travellers, and officials started to notice the drink,” Mehmet explains. “In 1585, Gianfrancesco Morosini, the Venetian bailo in Istanbul, reported to the Senate: ‘The Turks drink a black water as hot as they can suffer it, which is the infusion of a bean called cavee , which is said to possess the virtue of stimulating the mind’.” Some decades later, coffeehouses spread across Europe. “They would serve coffee prepared in a cezve or ibrik ,” Mehmet explains. “In fact, many 17th-century London coffeehouses took the name ‘The Turk’s Head’ to signal that they served coffee, and their penny tokens (small change issued by each house) were often stamped with the portrait of a sultan or a hand pouring from an ibrik .” The cezve’s cultural significance The brewing method at the centre of ancient Turkish coffee houses remains essentially unchanged today. Across countries like Greece, Armenia, Bulgaria, and Iran, coffee prepared in a cezve or ibrik carries a meaning that extends well beyond the cup. Turkish coffee is an inseparable part of our tradition of hospitality, Mehmet says. “It’s an expression of the sincere welcome we extend to guests.” In 2013, UNESCO recognised this significance by inscribing Turkish coffee culture and tradition on its Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity . One company has sat at the centre of this tradition for more than 150 years. Kurukahveci Mehmet Efendi , founded in Istanbul in 1871, is among the worlds oldest coffee roasters. Before the company launched, most consumers bought green beans and roasted and ground them at home. Kurukahveci Mehmet Efendi changed this by selling freshly roasted coffee ground to a very fine consistency needed for the cezve . The company now exports to 65 countries and has become synonymous with Turkish coffee worldwide. As Turkish coffee became more global, Western coffee drinkers moved away from unfiltered brewing. Mehmet explains: “Back then, grinding coffee by hand into the fine powder required to make a good cup of Turkish coffee was quite difficult and time-consuming. If not ground finely enough, the grounds will create an unpleasant, grainy feel on the tongue. “Filtering may have been a practical shortcut for European coffee brewers not acquainted with the traditional craft,” he adds. Turkish coffee emerges in specialty coffee Today, the cezve has become a point of interest in specialty coffee in Turkey and beyond. When prepared with care, baristas agree that Turkish coffee produces body and flavour intensity that paper filters would otherwise remove. Precise water temperatures, single origin beans, and controlled heat application have brought a new level of technical rigour to the ancient brewing method. In addition to Turkey’s thriving specialty coffee scene , where many modern cafés brew with the cezve , the World Cezve/Ibrik Championship has helped formalise this shift. The competition has placed the cezve alongside pour over methods used at the World Brewers Cup. The first edition was held in 2009 in Cologne, Germany, attracting competitors from Greece, Romania, Turkey, and Italy. However, many national Specialty Coffee Association chapters initially struggled to find enough competitors to justify hosting the event. After Greece’s Stavros Lamprinidis won the 2014 competition in Rimini, Italy, he helped revise the rules to attract more people to compete. Since then, baristas from Turkey, Poland, Sweden, Hungary, the UAE, Saudi Arabia, France, and Ukraine have taken part. World Cezve/Ibrik Championship competitors must prepare two drinks (a simple cezve/ibrik and a signature cezve/ibrik ) within 15 minutes. The competition also encourages baristas to bring their own cultural twist or flair to their routine, helping keep the tradition of Turkish coffee alive. How Turkish coffee continues to evolve The traditional copper pot heated over sand remains the preferred way to prepare Turkish coffee, but electric machines have also emerged, making the brewing method accessible to more people. Since UNESCO inscribed Turkish coffee culture on its list of Intangible Cultural Heritage in 2013, a new generation of coffee drinkers has been taking note, Mehmet says. While most Turkish coffee lovers will still prefer to use a traditional copper cezve , there are now also several good Turkish coffee machines that prepare a reliably good cup. From 14 to 17 May at the 2026 London Coffee Festival, Kurukahveci Mehmet Efendi will exhibit at booth G43, inviting attendees to experience the tradition and ritual of Turkish coffee firsthand. The cezve ’s journey from Turkish coffeehouses to the global specialty coffee competition stage is a testament to its enduring appeal. Whether brewed in a copper pot or a modern electric machine, Turkish coffee remains a true expression of hospitalit

textoCompleto: Coffee grinders are evolving, but what do baristas actually need from them? - Perfect Daily Grind --> ESPAÑOL ENGLISH PORTUGUÊS English Español Português Search for: Search Button SUBSCRIBE Skip to content Perfect Daily Grind > Barista > Coffee grinders are evolving, but what do baristas actually need from them? Share: Estimated reading time: 7 minutes Key takeaways Grinder speed and accuracy are equally important for baristas. Grind-by-weight technology is now an industry standard rather than a premium feature. Baristas need equipment that supports their workflow, not complicates it. Accurate, quick dosing allows baristas to trust their grinders. Coffee grinder technology has never been more sophisticated. From accurate gravimetric dosing to stepless micrometric adjustments and espresso machine sync systems, modern grinders offer baristas a level of precision and control not attainable until recent years. In turn, skilled baristas can unlock a coffee’s full flavour potential – improving their technical knowledge, enhancing the consumer experience, and helping cafés secure loyal customers. But as grinders become smarter, with more advanced features and interfaces, an important question emerges: Are they actually meeting baristas needs? “The shift toward grind-by-weight is now well established. The real challenge lies in developing a system that delivers high precision without compromising speed or workflow efficiency,” says Denis Girardi , the Sales Director at Macap . “Beyond the essential requirements – robust performance, ease of use, and consistency – two parameters are critical: dosing speed and accuracy.” Without both these factors, barista workflow is easily disrupted. If the grinder can’t keep pace with orders or produce accurate results, wait times increase, customer satisfaction drops, and coffee quality suffers. As the demands placed on baristas increase, they need grinders that aren’t just smart and intuitive, but also fast, consistent, and reliable. You may also like our article on the most valuable skills that every barista needs . Even advanced grinders need to get the basics right For most of specialty coffee’s history, espresso machines set the pace of equipment innovation. But over the last few years, grinders have caught up. Today, more feature touchscreens, pre-programmable grind settings, and connectivity that syncs with espresso machines – sharing key data and automatically adjusting grind size to meet recipe requirements. Given that many consider grinders the most important piece of equipment, these technological advances are crucial for improving coffee quality. But for many baristas, the conversation around grinder technology circles back to the foundations of great workflow: speed, accuracy, reliability, and consistency. “Our R&D efforts prioritise solutions that strike a balance between technological innovation and ease of use,” says Daniele Colizza , a R&D consultant who partners with Macap . “We introduce new technologies only when they offer a genuine advancement over established solutions, and most importantly, when they demonstrate superior performance and proven reliability in real operating conditions.” Alongside a full website refresh, Macap recently launched its new Supra 68 G and Supra 83 G Series grinders, both of which integrate grind-by-weight capabilities that support workflow efficiency. Macap will showcase the grinders at stand G07 at the 2026 London Coffee Festival from 14 to 17 May. Daniele explains that grinder technology needs to detect the smallest variations to ensure precise, accurate, and consistent results. “The Supra G can distinguish between portafilters within the same category, even when there are slight weight variations between them,” he says. “Our extremely fast and precise weighing system, combined with advanced software algorithms, makes this possible.” Without this capability, baristas would need to manually check doses, particularly during a busy service when portafilters are swapped quickly, and small inconsistencies can quickly impede workflow. Different coffee shops have different needs Today, the diversity of coffee shops is huge, and each café operates within its own set of conditions. Water quality varies by location; staff turnover means changing barista skills; physical layout influences workflow; and the customer base brings its own expectations. All these factors directly shape how baristas work. But for any coffee shop, speed of service is key. Research conducted by University College London found that the average customer is prepared to wait in line for 5 minutes and 54 seconds before considering leaving . The study also found that after waiting for five minutes, customer satisfaction can fall from 95% to 85%. “The biggest benefit of Macap’s Supra G grinder is how quickly they tare the portafilter and dose coffee,” says Jon Townsend , Director and Certified AST Trainer at The Institute of Coffee . “You save two to three seconds per shot, which compounds into a significant amount of time saved over the course of an entire day.” Cleanliness is also a major concern for any coffee shop, especially in high-traffic locations, where busy rushes can result in unclean, disorganised workstations. “With the Supra G grinders, we optimised the trajectory and fall speed of the coffee grounds and the positioning of the portafilter on the fork with a centring Coffee Targeting System mechanism,” Daniele explains. “During setup, the user can precisely adjust the drop point to ensure that the ground coffee lands perfectly in the centre of the portafilter. “Additionally, the integrated anti-clumping flapper prevents the formation of clumps and eliminates electrostatic effects, both of which contribute to a cleaner workspace,” he adds. “Baristas also have easy access to the grinding chamber through the Fast Opening System, making routine cleaning or replacement straightforward and efficient.” Meeting coffee shops where they are at Over the last decade or so, grind-by-weight systems have shifted from a premium differentiator to an industry expectation. But not all grinders are created equal, and what works in a low-volume specialty coffee shop may not be sufficient for a high-traffic chain in a busy location. For high-volume cafés serving 400 to 500 coffees a day, grinder features like consistency and accuracy are paramount,” Jon says. “You need that control to maintain a specific recipe within a tight timeframe.” Denis explains Macap’s Supra G grinders are designed to maintain dosing accuracy across all coffee shop environments. “The interaction between motor power, burr size and material, and the design of the grinding chamber ensures that performance remains stable, allowing the grinder to maintain consistent dosing accuracy even under continuous, high-speed operation throughout the day. “In many commercial grinders, dosing speed and weighing accuracy tend to be inversely proportional: increasing precision can slow down service, while prioritising speed can reduce accuracy,” he adds. “A well-implemented system is one that successfully balances these variables.” Earning baristas’ trust As grinders incorporate more high-tech features, they’re only as valuable as the baristas trust in them. One area where grinder technology has historically fallen short is scale accuracy, and the disruption to workflow and espresso consistency is significant. “‘You get what you read means that once a target dose is set, the system transparently displays the actual measured weight within its tolerance range, without artificially rounding the value to match the programmed dose,” Denis explains. “This approach prioritises transparency over superficial precision, allowing baristas to fully trust the data they see.” Speed is equally important. Technology that slows a baristas workflow through sluggish interfaces, delayed feedback, or complex processes is essentially working against them. By speeding up the taring and

textoCompleto: Coffee News Recap, 8 May: EUDR set to include instant coffee, Honduras expected to produce over 6 million bags for 2026/27 harvest & other stories - Perfect Daily Grind --> ESPAÑOL ENGLISH PORTUGUÊS English Español Português Search for: Search Button SUBSCRIBE Skip to content Perfect Daily Grind > Weekly Round-Up > Coffee News Recap, 8 May: EUDR set to include instant coffee, Honduras expected to produce over 6 million bags for 2026/27 harvest & other stories Share: Every Friday, Perfect Daily Grind rounds up the top coffee industry news from the week. Editor’s note The EUDR has covered coffee in almost all its forms since the regulation was first proposed, but instant coffee was notably absent from its scope. This omission created a gap that companies could technically exploit: a business could source, process, and package instant coffee entirely outside Europe, then import the finished product into the EU without triggering deforestation checks. The European Commission has now proposed to close the gap. Its latest draft delegated regulation proposes adding soluble coffee to the EUDRs scope, arguing that leaving it out creates a fragmented and incoherent approach for the coffee sector and may result in the relocation rather than the elimination of deforestation risk. The European Coffee Federation described the proposal as an important step towards a more coherent and workable framework for the sector. The draft is currently open for public feedback until 1 June 2026. The timing is crucial. The regulation is scheduled to take effect on 30 December 2026 for larger companies, and 30 June 2027 for smaller ones. Businesses handling instant coffee now face the same due diligence requirements as those working with green and roasted coffee. For large multinationals, the compliance burden is likely to be significant. Top stories of the week Mon, 4 May – Honduran coffee production is projected to reach 6.03 million bags by 2026/27 harvest . A new USDA report attributes the positive outlook to enhanced plant nutrition programmes, the positive phase of biennial production cycles, and the steady expansion of coffee-growing land areas. (Tegucigalpa, Honduras) Mon, 4 May – Backlash against WCC Chinese Taipei name change . On 28 April, following Bala’s WLAC win, the World Coffee Championships replaced all mentions of “Taiwan” with “Chinese Taipei” on its website, citing alignment with international sporting protocols. Members of the Taiwanese coffee community have rejected the decision, saying it erases Taiwan’s sovereignty and identity. (Taipei, Taiwan) Tue, 5 May – Yunnan plans to challenge Minas Gerais coffee production within the next decade . Through state-backed expansion and the China-Europe Railway Express, the province aims to scale arabica production and exports, potentially lowering global prices and displacing small-scale farmers in other producing countries. (Yunnan, China) Wed, 6 May – European Commission proposes to include soluble coffee in EUDR . According to the Commission, not including instant coffee within the legislation creates a fragmented and incoherent approach for the coffee sector”. The draft legislation is open to public feedback until 1 June. (Brussels, Belgium) Wed, 6 May – Royal Cup completes acquisition of Farmer Brothers . The coffee and tea manufacturer and distributor purchased Farmer Brothers Coffee, a US roaster, wholesaler and distributor, for US$1.29 per share. Both companies share 250 years of combined industry experience. The combined firm will be led by Royal Cup CEO Chip Wann, following the exit of Farmer Brothers’ top executive team. (Fort Worth, Texas, US) Thu, 7 May – Vietnam’s coffee exports rise 15.8% in volume . Despite growth in export quantities in early 2026, total revenue declined due to price volatility. The country’s coffee sector faces ongoing pressure to balance production levels against fluctuations in global demand. (Hanoi, Vietnam) Thu, 7 May – Edgar Rivas is the 2026 Colombian Barista Champion . Rivas won the 20th edition of the national competition, with María Paulina Pulido in second place and Ronald in third. Rivas will represent Colombia in the 2026 World Barista Championship, to be held in Panama City from 23-25 October. (Bogotá, Colombia) Fri, 8 May – Third WoC Asia takes place in Bangkok, hosting the 2026 World Cup Tasters Championship . The event runs until 9 May at BITEC, featuring the Roaster Village, Producer Village, cuppings, lectures and Brew Bars. (Bangkok, Thailand) Industry news Mon, 4 May – Grounds for Health transitions Kenya programme to local ownership . After ten years in Nyeri, the group is handing over its cervical cancer initiatives to local authorities. Efforts will now shift to scaling work in Ethiopia and expanding into Latin America by 2027. (Nyeri, Kenya) Mon, 4 May – Blue Tokai raises US$21m in Series D funding round . The Indian specialty coffee chain secured funding, led by Anicut Capital, to fuel its global expansion efforts. Operating over 175 cafés, the firm reported a 50% year-on-year increase in revenue in the 2025 financial year. (Gurugram, India) Tue, 5 May – Best US cities for coffee dates ranked . As coffee shops remain one of the most popular destinations for first dates, Tawkify surveyed 1,000 single adults across the US and ranked venue data across 95 cities. New York City, Portland, Oregon, and Seattle ranked in the top three. (San Francisco, California, US) Tue, 5 May – Löfbergs releases 2025 sustainability results . The Swedish roaster reported a 73% reduction in climate impact since 2005. Key achievements include supporting 112,000 smallholder farmers and converting 12,700 hectares to organic production. The firm also launched peat-free soil made from upcycled coffee waste. (Karlstad, Sweden) Wed, 6 May – Berlin’s LAP Coffee plans expansion to over 20 stores this year . The value-focused chain targets new stores this year in Cologne, Düsseldorf, and Frankfurt, adding to its 30 existing outlets. Its low-price, grab-and-go model has sparked both local opposition and growing customer demand. (Berlin, Germany) Wed, 6 May – McCafé revamp signals shift in coffee chain competition . McDonald’s applies lessons from its discontinued CosMc’s concept to refresh McCafé, targeting Gen Z with a new line of colourful, flavour-led, and customisable beverages . (Chicago, Illinois, US) Thu, 7 May – Bean & Brand agency brings big-brand storytelling to specialty coffee . Veteran copywriter Nick Usborne, who previously worked at Apple and Disney, launched a boutique firm to help independent roasters develop authentic marketing strategies. (Montréal, Québec, Canada) Thu, 7 May – Stokes Coffee rebrands with illustrated ‘Stokes People’ character system . The fourth-generation Lincoln roaster has modernised its brand identity across packaging and wholesale channels, developed with Eat Marketing, while preserving its 124-year heritage. (Lincoln, UK) Thu, 7 May – ONA partners with Grid Coffee in roaster collaboration across Chinese cities . The Australian roaster’s competition coffees feature under a ‘same price as overseas’ model in Beijing, Chengdu, and Shenzhen, as part of Grid’s third ‘World‑Class Guest’ programme. (Beijing, China) Fri, 8 May – Oval Coffee scales US distribution . The NYC roaster partnered with Whisha Specialty Coffee Distributors to expand its West Coast footprint. The deal brings Oval’s specialty coffee offerings to major retailers like Whole Foods and Target, starting in California. (New York City, New York, US) Fri, 8 May – Tim Hortons parent RBI posts Q1 profit surge as Tims logs 20th straight comps gain . Net income rose to US$338m, with revenue at US$2.26bn. Cold drinks surged as a key growth driver, yet traffic stalled in lunch categories. (Toronto, Canada) Fri, 8 May – Dutch Bros raises 2026 forecast after Q1 revenue jumps 31% . Same-store sales rose 8.3% on 5.1% higher transactions. The chain now expects at least 185 new shops and over US$2bn in ann

textoCompleto: Can cafés actually reduce costs by roasting their own coffee? - Perfect Daily Grind --> ESPAÑOL ENGLISH PORTUGUÊS English Español Português Search for: Search Button SUBSCRIBE Skip to content Perfect Daily Grind > Roasting Fundamentals > Can cafés actually reduce costs by roasting their own coffee? Share: Estimated reading time: 8 minutes Key takeaways Rising costs are pushing cafés to diversify their revenue streams. In-store roasting helps cafés control margins and reduce their reliance on wholesale suppliers. Compact roasting equipment makes in-house roasting accessible for smaller businesses. Custom products can attract both retail and wholesale clients. With rising costs across the board, cafés are under more pressure than ever. Green coffee prices reached their highest recorded levels in early 2025, exceeding US$4/lb . However, while raw material costs are important, they account for only a fraction of a coffee shop’s total expenses. Rent, wages, packaging, energy, and milk have all experienced significant price hikes since the pandemic, and these increases inevitably impact coffee shop prices. “Rising leases, rent, and overheads are becoming a real burden for most coffee business owners,” says Michael Quigley, the co-founder of Quigley Coffee Co . “What we have learned since starting our business almost five years ago is that combining creativity with diversifying our income was the only way we would survive in this cutthroat economy. “We had to look at what would be an asset to us, as well as our loyal customers who are dealing with the same issues we are.” Café operators are being pushed to diversify their products and services, building new revenue streams that can withstand market volatility and hurdles. For some, roasting in-house diversifies income, helps manage costs, and builds a more curated brand identity. You may also like our article on why roasters are demanding more from their machines . Why cafés need to manage their costs Despite a record 182.5 million 60kg bags predicted for the 2026/27 coffee harvest , arabica prices remain elevated, and uncertainty continues to shape the global coffee market. According to a March 2025 UN FAO report, it will take nearly a year for consumers to fully feel the effects of price spikes . More concerning still, the report estimates that the residual effects of these price rises will last for four years, meaning the market disruption of early 2025 will have a lasting impact on the industry. Consumers will inevitably adapt their purchasing behaviours in response. Many are likely to switch to more affordable options, visit coffee shops less frequently, and view specialty coffee as an occasional luxury rather than a daily routine. This creates both challenges and opportunities for café operators. Coffee shops will need to find innovative ways to cater to shifting preferences, develop new strategies to attract customers, and diversify their revenue streams. One of the most effective ways to do this is to roast and sell their own coffee. Following the emergence of micro roasters in the 2010s, in-store roasting has become more popular . Once costly and complex, the practice has become much more viable with the launch of more advanced, compact, and accessible equipment. “Beyond our brick-and-mortar café in Sanford, Florida, we run a full mobile espresso cart service for events, such as weddings, corporate events, and parties,” Michael Quigley says. “We started roasting in-house with the Bellwether Shop Roaster . The all-electric, ventless, and automatic countertop machine doesn’t require gas lines or construction and can roast up to 1.5kg per batch, with three to four roasts per hour.” How in-store roasting can benefit cafés In-store roasting offers café operators more control over their supply chains. Instead of relying on wholesalers, coffee shops can source and roast their own coffee, thereby better managing costs. “We were buying coffee from two different roasters at the time, and we weren’t sure if we should start roasting our own coffee,” says Michael Safarov , founder of Teremok Coffee & Desserts . “But costs started slowly creeping up. When we first opened, we paid US$7/lb, and now, from the same roaster, it’s almost US$15/lb. “With business expenses and insurance going up, you have to raise your prices to survive. You cant just eat your margin by 10%,” he adds. “Another one of our suppliers is located three hours away. Because were in a small town far from any other places, its hard for us to find a reliable roaster.” Roasting in-house helps cafés manage their margins and inventories more closely. The Bellwether Shop Roaster , for example, allows businesses to roast 20 kg in up to 4.5 hours, enabling even greater daily output depending on operating hours. “I can roast while Im working,” Michael Safarov says. “It takes me 30 seconds to a minute to load the green coffee, then continue baking muffins, counting inventory, or completing payroll, so it made sense.” Additionally, the Continuous Roasting Upgrade enables operators to load sufficient green coffee for up to 13 consecutive batches with minimal intervention. This means that, in a standard nine-hour shift, users could roast up to 40kg with only a few minutes of labour. “We started off slow, and then we kept going,” Michael Safarov says. “We replaced our decaf in our espresso bar, then drip, and then cold brew. We’re saving about US$754 every week, which is roughly US$40,000 per year. In less than six months, we’ve already paid off the machine.” Bellwether’s ROI calculator enables coffee business operators to better understand the costs and benefits of in-store roasting. The calculator also factors in variables such as per-pound savings, cost per cup, CO2 emissions reductions, and time to break even, providing a clearer picture of operational impact. Revenue diversification beyond coffee Beyond the added benefit of selling more coffee at higher margins, in-house roasting can help cafés build a more robust, curated brand identity. Coffee shops can offer greater product differentiation through custom and signature roast profiles and blends, unique to their business, helping them attract both B2B and B2C clients. “Roasting in-store with the Bellwether Shop Roaster has opened entirely new lines of business for us,” says Tracy Powell, the owner and co-founder of Momentum Coffee . “We now sell to cafés, restaurants, hotels, and office buildings. “Our signature blends have become customer favourites, giving us recurring sales online and in stores,” he adds. “Private-label roasting is another high-margin, low-churn business that continues to grow.” For cafés, in-house roasting creates opportunities beyond just selling their own coffee. The practice can transform coffee shops into interactive, multisensory spaces that foster deeper connections between customers and the products they buy, thereby strengthening the business’ branding and marketing. But with up to 70% of customers reportedly spending more with brands they consider authentic , product and revenue diversification must align with brand values and personality. “You need to own your process; roast your own coffee, make your own products, and control what matters most,” Tracy says. “Ownership is resilience.” Over time, businesses can establish themselves as reliable roasters, emphasising consistency and quality, building a stronger, more sophisticated brand identity. This can open doors to selling merchandise and clothing, further strengthening the brand and adding another channel for customers and wholesale clients to support them. “Using the Shop Roaster has kept our Cost of Goods Sold within margin during the rise in the green coffee market,” Michael Quigley explains. “It also completely changed the structure of our business. The café alone couldn’t reliably cover the costs of our space, even with loyal customers, so roasting expanded our revenue. “Now, we roast for the café, our carts, and

titulo: Brazil has set the template for how to drive domestic coffee consumption

Brazil remains the worlds largest coffee producer, and its domestic consumption is keeping pace with that status. textoCompleto: Brazil has set the template for how to drive domestic coffee consumption - Perfect Daily Grind --> ESPAÑOL ENGLISH PORTUGUÊS English Español Português Search for: Search Button SUBSCRIBE Skip to content Perfect Daily Grind > Other Essentials > Brazil has set the template for how to drive domestic coffee consumption Share: Estimated reading time: 7 minutes Key takeaways Brazil is one of the largest coffee-producing and consuming markets in the world. Specialty coffee now makes up an estimated 5-10% of Brazils market. Major cities are driving demand for higher-quality, traceable coffee. Strong domestic consumption gives Brazils coffee sector resilience to export disruptions, most notably US tariffs. Brazil remains the worlds largest coffee producer, and its domestic consumption is keeping pace with that status. Global commodities trader StoneX recently projected a record 182.5 million bags for the 2026/27 harvest , with Brazil expected to lead the growth, driven by a 20.8% year-on-year increase in total output. Specialty coffee consumption is also rising within the country, growing 15% in 2025 alone . Specialty coffee now accounts for an estimated 5-10% of the total domestic market by volume, pointing to a broader shift in consumer behaviour towards higher-quality options. The countrys long history of coffee production, well-established supply chains, and sustained investment in infrastructure have created the conditions for a strong domestic drinking culture. “For more than two centuries, coffee has shaped our economy, culture, and national identity. It’s part of daily life across every social class, from rural farms to major cities,” says Thiago (Rasta) Dias , a fifth-generation coffee producer, roaster, and cupper in Brazil. “Coffee represents accessibility and daily ritual. Our strong domestic consumption gives the sector resilience and stability.” Ultimately, Brazil shows that major producing countries arent limited to supplying the world; they can also build substantial coffee cultures at home. You may also like our article on how to roast Brazilian coffee . Brazils coffee culture was born from production Brazils rise as the worlds biggest coffee producer directly shaped its culture of domestic consumption. The first coffee plant is said to have arrived in Brazil in the 1700s, when Francisco de Melo Palheta, a Portuguese nobleman, planted it in the state of Pará . Coffee production quickly spread, primarily for the domestic market, but as coffee consumption increased in Europe, coffee plantations began to appear in Minas Gerais, Rio de Janeiro, and São Paulo to maintain supplies. At the time, Brazil accounted for only 20% of global coffee production, but it was the country’s main export. Yet by the mid-1900s, Brazil’s production had grown to account for 80% of global volumes. Coffee estates spread throughout regions such as Minas Gerais and Espírito Santo, growing both arabica and robusta. Today, Brazil is expected to produce record volumes of coffee. According to a recent Reuters article, a survey of 758 Brazilian coffee producers shows the 2026/27 harvest up 11.5% to 71.4 million bags . The country’s coffee consumption is also increasing. Brazilian Coffee Industry Association (ABIC) reported that domestic coffee consumption increased by 1.1% from November 2023 to October 2024, totalling almost 22 million bags . Early cafés in Brazil popularised the cafezinho , a drink prepared by boiling coffee with hot water and sugar, then straining it through cotton cloth. Dark roast profiles and sugar masked overly bitter flavours, as most higher-quality Brazilian coffee was exported at the time. But consumer awareness of quality started to increase, supported by the government and industry stakeholders. The Coffee Quality Programme , launched in 2004 by the Brazilian Coffee Industry Association (ABIC), helped improve consumer access to coffee quality verification systems. As a result, Brazilian consumers could engage with coffee in a new light and understand its nuance. A thriving specialty coffee scene A USDA Foreign Agricultural Service March 2026 report shows strong potential in Brazil’s specialty coffee sector , noting a rise in domestic “appreciation” and production of specialty coffee. But the country’s specialty coffee industry has been growing for decades. In 1991, the Brazilian Specialty Coffee Association launched , supporting producers, exporters, roasters, and café owners. Eight years later, Alliance for Coffee Excellence launched its first Cup of Excellence programme in Brazil , encouraging further investment in quality at farm level. “Over the last 15 to 20 years, specialty coffee has grown significantly in Brazil,” Thiago explains. “Consumers increasingly care about origin, varieties, processing, and the sensory experience of coffee.” As with many other consumer markets, major cities in Brazil, including São Paulo, Rio de Janeiro, and Belo Horizonte, drive interest in higher-quality coffee. Roasters like Coffee Lab , launched in 2009 in the Vila Madalena neighbourhood of São Paulo, were among the first to raise awareness of coffee quality and flavour. “Today, Brazil combines tradition and innovation. Filter coffee remains the most popular brewing method in Brazil,” Thiago says. “However, espresso-based beverages, capsule machines, and pour over brewing methods have gained significant traction in homes, offices, and cafés.” Brazil’s status on the global competition stage has also supported its homegrown coffee culture. In 2023, Boram Um became the first Brazilian competitor to win the World Barista Championship . He used a Panamanian Gesha, blended with a Pink Bourbon grown on his family farm in Brazil. Boram’s WBC victory marked a significant milestone for Brazil, highlighting its growing domestic consumption. In addition to Fazenda Um, the family also runs Um Coffee Co. shops throughout São Paulo, driving demand for locally-grown high-quality coffee even further. How could specialty coffee evolve in Brazil? Boram’s WBC win cast new light on Brazil’s specialty coffee scene on a global scale. Traditionally, Brazil has been known for delivering quantity and volume rather than exceptionally high-quality coffee. “Today, Brazil doesnt choose between volume and quality. From commodity to specialty, from scale to excellence, we deliver both,” Thiago says. “And this balance is what defines Brazils strength in the global coffee market.” Additionally, a robust domestic market enables producers to participate more actively on the consumer side, helping retain more value in the country’s coffee sector. “Brazilian producers are also responsible for fostering this growth in consumption, not just as farmers, but as entrepreneurs,” Thiago says. “They manage their farms like businesses, analysing data, climate risks, investing heavily in quality, post-harvest, crop management, and above all, seeking differentiation in the market.” He adds that for specialty coffee to continue growing, there needs to be more investment in the country’s infrastructure: “We need to increase consumer education and awareness, and develop stronger cooperatives and associations that support quality improvement and market access.” Brazil’s strength in the global coffee market was tested in early 2025, when US tariffs created serious pressure on Brazilian exports. With more than 16% of coffee exports destined for the US, the 50% tariff on Brazilian goods posed a significant challenge . Many US roasters, unable to absorb the high upfront costs, looked for alternative origins. Brazilian exporters, meanwhile, unable to lower their prices, redirected shipments to Europe and Asia. Coffee and other agricultural exports were ultimately exempted from the tariffs, but the situation ultimately influenced how Brazil approaches its domestic market. Now, more producers and exporters are weighing whether to retain more coffee at home rather than depend on export revenue. Brazils coffee sector op

Inevitably, this places more pressure on baristas than ever before. “Today, customers know much more about coffee than they did just five years ago,” says Tom Jenkins, a barista in Pr

textoCompleto: What are the most valuable skills every barista needs? - Perfect Daily Grind --> ESPAÑOL ENGLISH PORTUGUÊS English Español Português Search for: Search Button SUBSCRIBE Skip to content Perfect Daily Grind > Barista > What are the most valuable skills every barista needs? Share: Estimated reading time: 6 minutes Key takeaways Customer knowledge has raised the standard for every barista interaction. Automation changes the nature of the role, not just its difficulty. Hospitality and communication skills are now as important as technical ability. Treating barista roles as careers, not stopgaps, reduces staff turnover. Coffee shops have changed considerably over the last decade. Menus have become more complex, hiring and retaining skilled staff has become harder , and customers are more informed and demanding. Inevitably, this places more pressure on baristas than ever before. “Today, customers know much more about coffee than they did just five years ago,” says Tom Jenkins, a barista in Preston, UK. “Many now follow roasters on social media, know the difference between natural and washed processing, and have opinions about different plant milks. “While this level of knowledge may lessen the burden of ‘educating’ consumers, it raises the stakes for every interaction,” he adds. “A barista who can’t speak confidently about the menu or struggles to handle a specific order request isn’t performing at their best.” The growing demand placed on baristas reflects something positive: the role is now taken more seriously as a profession, rather than as a “stopgap” job. But it also reinforces the need for the coffee industry to acknowledge how much harder it has become to work as a barista in recent years. A January 2026 UK government report found that the main concerns for national hospitality businesses were energy prices, taxation, and inflation of goods and services . To cope with these challenges, many cafés are asking more of the staff they retain. “Baristas are now expected to multitask across a wider range of duties and take greater ownership of the customer experience,” Tom says. “Now, the most valuable skills that baristas need extend well beyond making drinks and providing good customer service. Communication, initiative, adaptability, and attention to detail are essential.” You may also like our article on how to attract and retain the best baristas and roasters . A changing industry means baristas prioritise different skills The hospitality industry has long struggled with high staff turnover. The US Bureau of Labor Statistics reports that the sector has the highest turnover rate in the country, at over 70% . A separate study found that nearly 3 million US hospitality workers left their jobs in early 2024 , a figure 204% above the national average quit rate. Meanwhile, in the UK, recent data shows that around 6% of hospitality workers leave their jobs every month . Most of these issues stem from how job roles like baristas are perceived. When treated as temporary rather than a long-term, professional position, employers invest less in training and development. This limits career progression, pushing baristas to find other work. Financial instability, poor benefits, long hours, and physically demanding conditions also exacerbate the problem. As coffee shops grapple with high staff turnover, barista shortages, and rising costs, more are turning to automation to support operations where needed , especially in high-volume settings. This has created a point of tension in specialty coffee – how do we improve efficiency and consistency without eroding craft and skill? The barista role demands technical precision: dialling in coffee, pouring crisp latte art, adjusting grind size, and brewing pour overs – all done consistently and under pressure. While they’re still essential today, the growing use of automation in coffee shops has shifted how baristas apply these skills on a daily basis. “The assumption is often that equipment like automated pour over brewers and milk foamers make the job simpler,” Tom says. “However, in practice, they change the nature of the work without necessarily reducing its difficulty. Baristas still need solid technical knowledge to use these machines properly. “When a machine manages extraction variables or milk streaming automatically, the baristas role shifts towards monitoring, maintenance, and quality control,” he adds. “Identifying when something is off, understanding why, and fixing the problem requires attention to detail and forward-thinking.” Hospitality and social skills matter more than ever With or without automation, continuously developing technical skills remains essential for any barista. But as equipment increasingly handles repetitive tasks, the focus increasingly shifts to customer service skills. Research from the Institute of Customer Service found that most consumers cite service quality as the main factor in whether they return to a business , ranking above price and product. “In this context, a technically perfect espresso served without attentiveness and engagement is lost,” Tom says. Emotional intelligence sits at the centre of excellent hospitality. Baristas work in environments that are often fast-paced, physically demanding, and full of customers, also under pressure to choose from ever-growing menus. The ability to read a situation accurately, respond to stressed customers without escalating tension, and maintain composure during a busy rush are professional skills that take time and experience to develop. Communication is just as important. Taking an order correctly when someone is distracted, clarifying preferences without making the customer feel questioned, upselling without applying pressure, and handling a complaint calmly showcase the ability to prioritise exceptional hospitality even in challenging situations. Brands like WatchHouse illustrate the success of a hospitality-first approach. After record revenues in 2025, the specialty coffee roaster is on track to reach 100 global sites by 2029 , backed by investors who see clear potential in its customer-focused model. Baristas greet guests at the door, seat them, walk them through the menu, and serve drinks tableside, turning a routine café visit into something closer to a dining experience. Research suggests that up to 78% of millennials prefer spending money on memorable experiences over material goods. For specialty coffee shops, strong hospitality is one of the most direct ways to justify premium pricing and build lasting customer loyalty – and more baristas are recognising this in their day-to-day job roles. Finding the right employers Although elevated service is becoming a key differentiating factor in specialty coffee, it’s not without its challenges. The barista role is already demanding, so the added pressure to deliver consistently exceptional customer service only increases the workload. For coffee shops looking to elevate service, this means investing in barista training, support, and career progression. Exceptional hospitality will only work if staff feel supported, engaged, respected, and acknowledged. For anyone actively looking for barista roles or broader opportunities across the coffee sector, PDG Jobs is a practical resource. The platform lists positions in several countries, ranging from entry-level barista roles to senior roles in coffee and hospitality management. The barista role has changed significantly. Technical skill still matters, but it now sits alongside communication, adaptability, and emotional intelligence as equally important responsibilities. “Coffee shops that invest in their staff and treat barista positions as genuine careers support this shift in skill, Tom concludes. In doing so, they will be able to retain the skilled, passionate people who help their businesses succeed. Looking for new positions in the coffee industry? Check out PDG Jobs here . titulo: It’s harder than ever to stand out at competitions: Baristas need to find an edge

Winning routines reflect what is most relevant to the coffee industry today. Competitions like the World Barista Championship have always rewarded innovation and excellence.
